content:
# Luke Chapter 4 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 88-89 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_004_page_001.jpg, LUK_004_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And Jesus, full of the Holy Spirit, returned from the Jordan and was led by the Spirit into the wilderness

**2** for forty days, being tempted by the devil. And he ate nothing in those days, and when they were completed, he hungered.

**3** And the devil said to him, "If you are the Son of God, command this stone to become bread."

**4** And Jesus answered him, "It is written, 'Man shall not live by bread alone.'"

**5** And leading him up, he showed him all the kingdoms of the world in a moment of time.

**6** And the devil said to him, "To you I will give all this authority and their glory, for it has been delivered to me, and to whomever I wish I give it.

**7** If you, therefore, will worship before me, it shall all be yours."

**8** And Jesus answered and said to him, "It is written, 'You shall worship the Lord your God, and him only shall you serve.'"

**9** And he led him to Jerusalem and set him upon the pinnacle of the temple and said to him, "If you are the Son of God, cast yourself down from here,

**10** for it is written, 'He shall command his angels concerning you, to guard you,'

**11** and, 'On their hands they shall bear you up, lest you strike your foot against a stone.'"

**12** And Jesus answered and said to him, "It has been said, 'You shall not put the Lord your God to the test.'"

**13** And when the devil had completed every temptation, he departed from him until an opportune time.

**14** And Jesus returned in the power of the Spirit to Galilee, and a report went out through all the surrounding region concerning him.

**15** And he taught in their synagogues, being glorified by all.

**16** And he came to Nazareth, where he had been brought up, and he entered, according to his custom, on the day of the sabbaths into the synagogue, and he stood up to read.

**17** And the scroll of the prophet Isaiah was given to him, and having unrolled the scroll, he found the place where it was written:

**18** "The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor; he has sent me to proclaim release to the captives, and recovery of sight to the blind, to set at liberty those who are oppressed,

**19** to proclaim the acceptable year of the Lord."

**20** And having rolled up the scroll, he gave it back to the attendant and sat down. And the eyes of all in the synagogue were fixed upon him.

**21** And he began to say to them, "Today this scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing."

**22** And all bore witness to him and marveled at the words of grace proceeding from his mouth, and they said, "Is not this the son of Joseph?"

**23** And he said to them, "Surely you will say this parable to me: 'Physician, heal yourself. Whatever we heard was done in Capernaum, do here also in your own homeland.'"

**24** And he said, "Truly I say to you, no prophet is acceptable in his own homeland.

**25** But in truth I say to you, there were many widows in Israel in the days of Elijah, when the heaven was shut up for three years and six months, while a great famine came upon all the land,

**26** and to none of them was Elijah sent except to Zarephath of Sidon, to a woman who was a widow.

**27** And there were many lepers in Israel in the time of Elisha the prophet, and none of them was cleansed except Naaman the Syrian."

**28** And all in the synagogue were filled with wrath when they heard these things,

**29** and they rose up and cast him out of the city and led him to the brow of the hill upon which their city had been built, so as to throw him down the cliff.

**30** But he, passing through the midst of them, went on his way.

**31** And he went down to Capernaum, a city of Galilee, and was teaching them on the sabbaths.

**32** And they were astonished at his teaching, for his word was with authority.

**33** And in the synagogue there was a man having a spirit of an unclean demon, and he cried out with a loud voice,

**34** "Ha! What is there between us and you, Jesus of Nazareth? Have you come to destroy us? I know who you are — the Holy One of God."

**35** And Jesus rebuked him, saying, "Be silent and come out of him." And the demon, having thrown him into the midst, came out of him, having done him no harm.

**36** And amazement came upon all, and they spoke to one another, saying, "What is this word? For with authority and power he commands the unclean spirits, and they come out."

**37** And a report concerning him went out into every place of the surrounding region.

**38** And having risen from the synagogue, he entered into the house of Simon. Now the mother-in-law of Simon was afflicted with a great fever, and they made request of him concerning her.

**39** And standing over her, he rebuked the fever, and it left her; and immediately she rose up and began to serve them.

**40** And when the sun was setting, all who had any that were sick with various diseases brought them to him, and he, laying his hands on each one of them, healed them.

**41** And demons also came out from many, crying out and saying, "You are the Son of God." And rebuking them, he did not allow them to speak, because they knew him to be the Christ.

**42** And when day came, he went out and journeyed to a desolate place; and the crowds sought him and came to him and tried to prevent him from departing from them.

**43** But he said to them, "It is necessary for me to proclaim the good news of the kingdom of God to the other cities also, for I was sent for this purpose."

**44** And he was preaching in the synagogues of Judea.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Codex Sinaiticus)

1. **Page layout:** Luke 4 begins in column 3 of page 88 (folio 243v) at verse 1, continuing the text from the genealogy of chapter 3. The chapter extends across all four columns of page 89 (folio 244r), concluding at verse 44 in column 4.

2. **Verse 1 — "led by the Spirit" (HGETO EN TW PNEUMATI):** Sinaiticus reads "was led by the Spirit," using the imperfect passive, indicating continuous leading throughout the wilderness period rather than a single initiating act.

3. **Verse 4 — Shorter reading:** Sinaiticus reads OYK EP ARTW MONW ZHCETAI O ANQRWPOC, "Man shall not live by bread alone," omitting the second clause found in many later manuscripts ("but by every word of God"). This shorter reading, also supported by Codex Vaticanus, is widely regarded as original, with the longer form being a scribal harmonization with Matthew 4:4 and Deuteronomy 8:3. The LOGOS follows Sinaiticus here.

4. **Verse 5 — Temptation order:** Sinaiticus preserves Luke's distinctive temptation sequence, in which the kingdoms temptation comes second and the temple pinnacle third, differing from Matthew's order. This is the consistent Lukan order across all major manuscripts.

5. **Verse 16 — "day of the sabbaths" (TH HMERA TWN CABBATWN):** Sinaiticus reads the genitive plural CABBATWN. The LOGOS renders this literally as "the day of the sabbaths," preserving the Sinaiticus form, though the expression functions as "the sabbath day."

6. **Verse 17 — "scroll" (BIBLION):** Rendered "scroll" rather than "book," since BIBLION in this context refers to a rolled papyrus or leather scroll, the standard synagogue format for prophetic texts in the first century.

7. **Verse 18 — Isaiah quotation (Isaiah 61:1-2):** Sinaiticus preserves Luke's form of the quotation, which combines Isaiah 61:1 with a phrase from Isaiah 58:6 ("to set at liberty those who are oppressed"). This composite quotation is characteristic of Luke's presentation.

8. **Verse 23 — "homeland" (PATRIC):** Sinaiticus uses PATRIC (homeland, native place) rather than a more specific term. Rendered "homeland" to preserve the broader connotation, which encompasses both the town of Nazareth and the region of one's origin.

9. **Verse 33 — "spirit of an unclean demon" (PNEUMA DAIMONIOY AKAQARTOY):** This distinctively Lukan expression combines "spirit," "demon," and "unclean" in a single phrase. The LOGOS preserves the full construction rather than simplifying to "unclean spirit" or "demon."

10. **Verse 34 — "Ha!" (EA):** Sinaiticus reads EA, an interjection of alarm or hostility. Rendered "Ha!" to convey the exclamatory force of the original rather than leaving it untranslated or smoothing it away.

11. **Verse 41 — "him to be the Christ":** Sinaiticus reads HDEISAN TON XRICTON AYTON EINAI, "they knew him to be the Christ." Some manuscripts read "they knew him to be the Christ, the Son of God" (not in Sinaiticus here). The LOGOS follows the Sinaiticus reading.

12. **Verse 44 — "Judea" (IOYDAIAC):** Sinaiticus reads "the synagogues of Judea," where some manuscripts read "Galilee" (GALILAIAC). The Sinaiticus reading is the more difficult (lectio difficilior), since the preceding context is set in Galilee. "Judea" here may refer broadly to the land of the Jews (i.e., all Jewish territory) rather than the southern province specifically. The LOGOS retains the Sinaiticus reading.

### Translation Philosophy

- **"Tempted" (PEIRAZOMENOC):** The Greek verb PEIRAZW carries the dual sense of "test" and "tempt." In verse 2, "tempted" is used because the devil is the agent; in verse 12, "put to the test" renders the same root in the quotation from Deuteronomy, where the sense is of testing God's faithfulness.
- **"Proclaim good news" (EYAGGELIZW):** Rendered as the verbal phrase "proclaim good news" rather than the single word "evangelize" (a transliteration) or the looser "preach." This preserves the active, proclamatory force of the Greek verb while making its content ("good news") explicit.
- **"Authority" (EXOYCIA):** Consistently rendered "authority" throughout the chapter (vv. 6, 32, 36), preserving the theological weight of this term, which in Luke denotes divinely granted power and right, whether claimed falsely by the devil or exercised genuinely by Jesus.
- **"Rebuked" (EPETIMHCEN):** Used consistently for Jesus' authoritative commands directed at fever (v. 39), demons (vv. 35, 41), preserving the forceful, commanding sense of the Greek, which implies sovereign authority over the forces of illness and evil.
